name: From Lozoyuela to Buitrago climbing the Lozoya river — Sierra de Guadarrama
name_original: De Lozoyuela a Buitrago remontando el río Lozoya — Sierra de Guadarrama
region_intro: Mountain bike trails around Lozoyuela-Navas-Sieteiglesias traverse a landscape characterized by rolling hills, meadows, and dehesas within the Sierra Norte de Madrid. The region features extensive trail networks, including routes along the Lozoya River and around significant reservoirs like El Villar and Pinilla. Riders encounter diverse terrain, from singletracks to technical sections, set against the backdrop of the Sierra de La Cabrera and the broader Sierra de Guadarrama. Distinctive granite rock formations also dot the area, offering varied scenery for mountain biking. Best mountain bike trails around Lozoyuela-Navas-Sieteiglesias The most popular mountain bike trail is From Lozoyuela to Buitrago climbing the Lozoya river — Sierra de Guadarrama, a 39.8 miles (64.1 km) difficult route that takes 5 hours 51 minutes to complete. This trail offers significant climbs and views along the Lozoya river. Another top favourite among local mountain bikers is Puente del Cura – Views of the Lozoya River loop from Sieteiglesias, a moderate 26.1 miles (42.0 km) path. This loop provides scenic views of the Lozoya River and surrounding natural features. Local mountain bikers also love the El Villar Reservoir Dam – View of the La Puebla River loop from Lozoyuela, a 48.5 miles (78.1 km) difficult trail leading through the area around El Villar Reservoir Dam, often completed in about 7 hours 41 minutes. Mountain biking around Lozoyuela-Navas-Sieteiglesias is defined by rolling hills, extensive reservoir systems, and the mountainous terrain of the Sierra de Guadarrama. The network offers options for different ability levels, from moderate loops to more challenging routes. The routes in Lozoyuela-Navas-Sieteiglesias are highly rated by the komoot community with an average score of 4.4 stars from over 100 reviews. More than 1600 mountain bikers have used komoot to explore Lozoyuela-Navas-Sieteiglesias's varied terrain.
region_faq:
Q: What kind of terrain can I expect on the mountain bike trails around Lozoyuela-Navas-Sieteiglesias?
A: The trails traverse a diverse landscape featuring rolling hills, meadows, and dehesas within the Sierra Norte de Madrid. You'll encounter extensive trail networks, including routes along the Lozoya River and around significant reservoirs like El Villar and Pinilla. Riders can expect everything from singletracks to technical sections, set against the backdrop of the Sierra de La Cabrera and the broader Sierra de Guadarrama. Distinctive granite rock formations also dot the area, offering varied scenery.

Q: How many mountain bike trails are available in the Lozoyuela-Navas-Sieteiglesias area?
A: There are over 25 mountain bike routes available in the Lozoyuela-Navas-Sieteiglesias area, offering a range of experiences for different skill levels. The komoot community has explored more than 27 unique tours here.

Q: Are there any easy or beginner-friendly mountain bike trails?
A: While the area is rich with moderate and difficult trails, there are no routes explicitly categorized as 'easy' in the guide data. Most trails are rated as moderate, making them suitable for riders with some experience, or difficult for those seeking a greater challenge. For example, &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/31518734" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Puente del Cura – Views of the Lozoya River loop from Sieteiglesias&lt;/a&gt; is a moderate 42 km path.

Q: What are the typical lengths and durations of mountain bike routes in this region?
A: The routes vary significantly in length and duration. Moderate trails can be around 40-50 km, taking approximately 3.5 to 4 hours. More difficult routes, such as &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/40006798" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;El Villar Reservoir Dam – View of the La Puebla River loop from Lozoyuela&lt;/a&gt;, can extend up to 78 km and take over 7 hours to complete, involving significant elevation changes.

Q: What natural features or landmarks can I see along the mountain bike trails?
A: You can discover several notable natural features and landmarks. The &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/2839855" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Cancho de la Cabeza Summit&lt;/a&gt; offers spectacular 360-degree views. You might also encounter the impressive &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/2791690" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;El Villar Reservoir and Gorge&lt;/a&gt;, or enjoy panoramic vistas from the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/5148338" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Pinilla Reservoir Viewpoint&lt;/a&gt;. The region is also home to the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/5437203" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Mojonavalle Forest&lt;/a&gt; and the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/5299820" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Canencia Pass&lt;/a&gt;, known for its scenic climbs.

Q: When is the best time of year to go mountain biking in Lozoyuela-Navas-Sieteiglesias?
A: The region is particularly vibrant in spring, when the scents of rockrose, thyme, and rosemary fill the air, enhancing the sensory experience. However, its diverse landscapes make it suitable for mountain biking across multiple seasons, with pleasant conditions often found in spring and autumn. Summer can be warm, while winter might bring cooler temperatures, especially at higher elevations.

Q: Are there any challenging trails for experienced mountain bikers?
A: Yes, the area offers several difficult trails for experienced riders. For instance, &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e1344913599/from-lozoyuela-to-buitrago-climbing-the-lozoya-river-sierra-de-guadarrama" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;From Lozoyuela to Buitrago climbing the Lozoya river — Sierra de Guadarrama&lt;/a&gt; is a difficult 64 km route with significant climbs. Another challenging option is the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/40006798" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;El Villar Reservoir Dam – View of the La Puebla River loop from Lozoyuela&lt;/a&gt;, a 78 km difficult trail with substantial elevation gain.

Q: What do other mountain bikers enjoy most about the trails in Lozoyuela-Navas-Sieteiglesias?
A: The area is highly rated by the komoot community, with an average score of 4.4 stars from over 100 reviews. Reviewers often praise the varied terrain, the scenic views of the Lozoya River and reservoirs, and the extensive network that caters to different ability levels. The combination of rolling hills, dehesas, and mountainous backdrops is frequently highlighted.

Q: Are there any loop trails available for mountain biking?
A: Yes, many of the mountain bike routes in the area are designed as loops, offering a convenient way to explore without retracing your steps. Examples include &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/31518734" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Puente del Cura – Views of the Lozoya River loop from Sieteiglesias&lt;/a&gt; and &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/23758236" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;El Villar Reservoir and Gorge – View of Buitrago del Lozoya loop from Lozoyuela&lt;/a&gt;.

Q: Can I find places to eat or stay near the mountain bike trails?
A: Lozoyuela-Navas-Sieteiglesias and its surrounding towns offer amenities for visitors. The region has cultural points of interest like the Church of San Pedro Apóstol and ethnographic museums, indicating the presence of local services, including cafes, pubs, and accommodation options for those looking to extend their stay.

Q: Are there any trails that offer views of the reservoirs?
A: Absolutely. The region is known for its interconnected reservoirs. Routes like &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/40006798" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;El Villar Reservoir Dam – View of the La Puebla River loop from Lozoyuela&lt;/a&gt; and &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/31551998" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Views of the Lozoya River – El Villar Reservoir Dam loop from Sieteiglesias&lt;/a&gt; specifically highlight views of the El Villar Reservoir. The Pinilla Reservoir also provides panoramic vistas, making it a scenic backdrop for your ride.
tips: This Roman bridge, dating back to the 16th century. I. BC, was key to the economic growth of the population of El Berrueco during the 16th century. XV d. C. Entrance town to the Archbishopric of Toledo from the port of Somosierra, connecting it with Talamanca del Jarama through the homonymous canyon, as well as Alcalá de Henares, Madrid and Uceda. This place full of transit history invites us to imagine ourselves on horseback while we cycle through it.

El Berrueco is the typical town at the foot of the Sierra de Guadarrama full of houses and granite structures. It is a transit site attached to the Atazar reservoir that has all the lodging and restaurant services. Numerous routes on foot and by bicycle originate from, go to, or pass through this town that leads, from the A1 highway, to the natural area of the Sierra Norte de Madrid.

Built in 1972 and belonging to the Isabel II canal, the El Atazar reservoir is the largest in Madrid, representing 46% of the community's embalmed water. Located in a rugged enclave full of slate, the landscape that surrounds the reservoir and that is born from its waters is spectacular, giving way to monumental natural routes and walks through its surrounding historical towns.

The Lozoya is an important tributary of the Jarama along whose 91 kilometer route six reservoirs have been built. Right at this point, the valley takes the shape of a canyon due to its proximity to the El Villar reservoir, where the course of the river narrows to a great extent.
This dam offers spectacular views. It is worth going there. Ideal for hiking, motorbike or road bike excursions. Next to the dam there is a small parking lot for about 10 cars.
Recreational area near the Embalse del Villar dam made up of a forest populated by pines, gall oaks, oaks and poplars. Ideal for crossing your paths.

The Puentes Viejas reservoir was built on the Lozoya River and is part of the water supply network known as Canal de Isabel II, which is still in operation after several centuries in use. It is also a panoramic site with very nice views.

On the banks of the Lozoya River at the beginning of the El Atazar reservoir we find this forest populated with pine, ash and Montpellier maple. Leafy, it creates a cozy natural intimacy that collects us in its bed as we pass through its paths.

Located on an old Muslim fortress reconquered in 1085 under the reign of Alfonso VI. The first testimonies of the castle come from the hand of Alfonso VII, who gave the properties to the dukes of Mendoza. These, in the s. XV, they would build a castle fortified by seven different towers, built with brick in the Mudejar style. The Marquis of Santillana and, later, Juana de Portugal and her daughter, Juana la Beltraneja, would reside there.
During the War of Independence, the French would use the fortress as a barracks, fleeing from it in 1813, destroying it in their escape, then beginning its deterioration.
It currently has a bullring inside and has been closed for restoration since 2016. It is a historical gem that should not be overlooked if you visit the Madrid town.

It is a unique town that preserves its complete wall, the only one in the region and declared a National Monument in 1931. Don't miss its Gothic-Mudejar style Alcázar with a clear Arab influence. A storybook and postcard town like few others.

Beautiful town in the Sierra de Madrid. The Alczar (castle), the very colorful Puente del Arrabal and the Church of Santa María del Castillo stand out.
It also has the Picasso Museum that brings together an important collection of the ceramic works of the Malaga painter.
Numerous bars, restaurant, supermarkets, and accommodation if necessary
In use during the postwar period to house prisoners of war who participated in the construction of the train life. in this way they reduced their sentences in exchange for days of work.

The old Lozoyuela station was located on the direct Madrid-Burgos railway line, operational between 1968 and 2011. Completed during the Franco dictatorship, it was one of the most tedious projects on the Spanish railway network, taking around 50 years to build. Today, we find various stations, fragments of track, tunnels and former prisons of those who built the line.
